Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2020

## - UNIFYING REGULARISATION METHODS FOR CONTIN UAL LEARNING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Continual Learning addresses the challenge of learning a number of different distributions sequentially. The goal of maintaining knowledge of earlier distributions without re-accessing them starkly conflicts with standard SGD training for artificial neural networks. An influential method to tackle this are so-called regularisation approaches. They measure the importance of each parameter for modelling a given distribution and subsequently protect important parameters from large changes. In the literature, three ways to measure parameter importance have been put forward and they have inspired a large body of follow-up work. Here, we present strong theoretical and empirical evidence that these three methods, Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC), Synaptic Intelligence (SI) and Memory Aware Synapses (MAS), are all related to the Fisher Information. Only EWC intentionally relies on the Fisher, while the other two methods stem from rather different motivations. We find that for SI the relation to the Fisher – and in fact its performance – is due to a previously unknown bias. Altogether, this unifies a body of regularisation approaches. It provides better explanations for the effectiveness of SI- and MASbased algorithms and offers more justified versions of these algorithms. From a practical viewpoint, our insights uncover conditions needed for different algorithms to work and allow predicting their relative performance in some situations as well as offering improvements.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Despite considerable progress, many gaps between biological and machine intelligence remain. Animals, for example, flexibly learn new tasks throughout their lives, while at the same time maintaining robust memories of previous knowledge. This ability conflicts with traditional training procedures for artificial neural networks, which overwrite previous skills when optimizing new tasks (McCloskey & Cohen, 1989; Goodfellow et al., 2013). The field of continual learning is dedicated to mitigate this crucial shortcoming of machine learning. It exposes neural networks to a sequence of distinct tasks. During training new tasks, the algorithm is not allowed to revisit old data, but should retain previous skills while remaining flexible enough to also acquire new knowledge.

An influential line of work to approach this challenge was introduced by Kirkpatrick et al. (2017), who proposed the first regularisation-algorithm, Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC). After training a task, EWC measures the importance of each parameter and introduces an auxiliary loss penalising large changes in important parameters. Naturally, this raises the question of how to measure this ‘importance’. While EWC uses the diagonal Fisher Information, two alternatives have been proposed: Synaptic Intelligence (SI, Zenke et al. (2017)) aims to attribute the decrease in loss during training to individual parameters and Memory Aware Synapses (MAS, Aljundi et al. (2018)) introduces a heuristic measure of output sensitivity. Together, these three approaches have inspired many further regularisation-based approaches, including combinations of them (Chaudhry et al., 2018), refinements (Huszár, 2018; Ritter et al., 2018; Chaudhry et al., 2018; Yin et al., 2020), extensions (Schwarz et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2018; Park et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020) and applications in different continual learning settings (Aljundi et al., 2019) as well as different domains of machine learning (Lan et al., 2019). Almost every new continual learning method compares to at least one of EWC, SI and MAS.
Despite their popularity and influence, basic practical and theoretical questions regarding these algorithms had previously been unanswered. Notably, it was unkown how similar these importance

measures are. Additionally, for SI and MAS (and their follow-ups) there was no solid theoretical understanding of their effectiveness. Here, we close both these gaps through a theoretical analysis confirmed by a series of carefully designed experiments on standard continual learning benchmarks. Our main findings can be summarised as follows:
- (a) We show that MAS is almost identical to the ‘Absolute Fisher’, a quantity similar to the Fisher Information.
- (b) We show that SI’s importance approximation is biased, that the bias is responsible for SI’s performance and that the bias, like MAS, is tightly linked to the Absolute Fisher.
- (c) Our fine-grained analysis leads to new baselines, including more justified versions of MAS and SI.

- (d) Together, (a) and (b) show that all three regularisation approaches (and their follow-ups) are closely linked to the Fisher Information. This unifies a large body of regularisation literature. It also gives a more solid theoretical justification for the effectiveness of SI- and MAS-based algorithms.
- (e) Our precise understanding of SI allows predicting and improving its performance in different situations and offers a better performing alternative.

## 2 RELATED WORK

The problem of catastrophic forgetting in neural networks has been studied for many decades (McCloskey & Cohen, 1989; Ratcliff, 1990; French, 1999). In the context of deep learning, it received more attention again (Goodfellow et al., 2013; Srivastava et al., 2013). All previous work we are aware of proposes new or modified algorithms to tackle continual learning. No attention has been directed towards understanding or unifying existing methods and we hope that our work will not remain the only effort of this kind. We now review the broad body of continual learning algorithms. Following (Parisi et al., 2019), they are often categorised into regularisation-, replay- and architectural approaches. Most _regularisation methods_ will be reviewed more closely in the next section. There we cover regularisation for standard neural nets, but the same idea has also been applied to bayesian neural networks (Nguyen et al., 2017; Swaroop et al., 2019). An alternative approach (Mirzadeh et al., 2020) does not penalize deviating from important parameters, but rather modifies training so that the network is robust to such deviations. _Replay methods_ refer to algorithms which either store a small sample or generate data of old distributions and use this data while training on new methods (Rebuffi et al., 2017; Lopez-Paz & Ranzato, 2017; Shin et al., 2017; Kemker & Kanan, 2017). These approaches can be seen as investigating how far standard i.i.d.-training can be relaxed towards the (highly non-i.i.d.) continual learning setting without losing too much performance. They are interesting, but usually circumvent the original motivation of continual learning to maintain knowledge _without_ accessing old distributions. Intriguingly, the most effective way to use old data appears to be simply replaying it, i.e. mimicking training with i.i.d. batches sampled from all tasks simultaneously (Chaudhry et al., 2019). _Architectural methods_ extend the network as new tasks arrive (Fernando et al., 2017; Li et al., 2019; Schwarz et al., 2018; Golkar et al., 2019; von Oswald et al., 2019). This can be seen as a study of how old parts of the network can be effectively used to solve new tasks and touches upon transfer learning. Typically, it avoids the challenge of integrating new knowledge into an existing networks. Finally, van de Ven & Tolias (2019); Hsu et al. (2018); Farquhar & Gal (2018) point out that different continual learning scenarios and assumptions with varying difficulty were used across the literature.[1]

## 3 REVIEW OF EXISTING REGULARISATION METHODS

## 3.1 FORMAL DESCRIPTION OF CONTINUAL LEARNING

In continual learning we are given _K_ datasets _D_ 1 _, . . . , DK_ sequentially. When training a neural net with _N_ parameters _θ ∈_ R _[N]_ on dataset _Dk_ , we have no access to the previously seen datasets _D_ 1: _k−_ 1. However, at test time the algorithms is tested on all _K_ tasks, usually the average accuracy is taken as measure of the algorithms performance, but see also Chaudhry et al. (2018).

Regularisation based approaches introduced in Kirkpatrick et al. (2017); Huszár (2018) protect previous memories by modifying the loss function _Lk_ related to dataset _Dk_ . Let us denote the parameters obtained after finishing training on task _k_ by _θ_[(] _[k]_[)] and let _ω_[(] _[k]_[)] _∈_ R _[N]_ be the parameters’ _importances_ . When training on task _k_ , regularisation methods use the loss

where _c >_ 0 is a hyperparameter. The first term _Lk_ is the standard (e.g. cross entropy) loss of task _k_ . The second term ensures that the parameters do not move away too far from their previous values. Usually, _ωi_[(] _[k]_[)] = _ωi_[(] _[k][−]_[1)] + _ωi_ , where _ωi_ is the importance for the most recently learned task _k_ .

## 3.3 REVIEW OF EWC, MAS, SI AND FOLLOW-UPS

**Elastic Weight Consolidation** (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017) uses the diagonal of the Fisher Information as importance measure. It is evaluated at the end of training a given task. To define the _Fisher Information Matrix F_ , we omit the task index _k_ and simply consider a dataset _X_ . For each datapoint _X ∈X_ the network predicts a probability distribution **q** _X_ over the set of labels _L_ , where we suppress dependence of _qX_ on _θ_ for simplicity of notation. We denote the predicted probability of class _y_ by **q** _X_ ( _y_ ) for each _y ∈ L_ . Let us assume that we minimize the negative log-likelihood log **q** _X_ ( _y_ ) and **[q]** _[X]_[(] _[y]_[)] write _g_ ( _X, y_ ) = _−[∂]_[lo][g] _∂θ_ for its gradient. Then the Fisher Information _F_ is given by

Taking only the **diagonal Fisher** means _ωi_ (EWC) = E _X∼X_ E _y∼_ **q** _X_ � _gi_ ( _X, y_ )[2][�] _._ The _Empirical Fisher Information_ replaces the expectation over _y ∼_ **q** _X_ by the (deterministic) label _y_ given by the labels of the dataset. We also define the _Predicted Fisher Information_ as taking the maximum-likelihood predicted label, i.e. the argmax of **q** _X_ ( _·_ ). When the network classifies images correctly and confidently, these three versions clearly become increasingly similar. Under the assumption that the learned label distribution **q** _X_ is the real label distribution the Fisher equals the Hessian of the loss (Martens, 2014; Pascanu & Bengio, 2013). Its use for continual learning has a bayesian, theoretical interpretation (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017; Huszár, 2018).

**Memory Aware Synapses** (Aljundi et al., 2018) heuristically argues that the sensitivity of the function output with respect ot a parameter should be used as the parameter’s importance. This sensitivity is evaluated after training a given task. The formulation in the paper suggests using the predicted probability distribution to measure parameter sensitivity. The probability distribution is the output of the neural network and the quantity that one aims to conserve during continual learning. Below we describe the precise importance this leads to. It was however brought to our attention that the MAS codebase seems to interpret the logits rather then the probability distribution as output of the neural network. In Appendix D we show that the resulting version of MAS has the same performance as the one described here, and, crucially, is also related to the Fisher Information.

To formalise the ‘contribution of a parameter’, let us denote the parameters at time _t_ by _θ_ ( _t_ ) and the loss by _L_ ( _t_ ). **Follow up work.** We presented a version of EWC due to Huszár (2018) and tested in (Chaudhry et al., 2018; Schwarz et al., 2018). It is theoretically more sound and was shown to perform better. ## 4 MEMORY AWARE SYNAPSES (MAS) APPROXIMATES ABSOLUTE FISHER

Here, we explain why – despite its different motivation – MAS is almost linearly dependent of the **diagonal Absolute Fisher** , a variant of the diagonal Fisher taking absolute values of gradients rather than squares. We first present the theory and then carry out careful empirical confirmations of the underlying assumptions. For a summary of the definitions of algorithms and baselines see Table B.1. In Appendix D we present analogous results for MAS based on logits rather than the predicted probability distribution, showing that it performs equally and, crucially, is also related to the Fisher.

## 4.1 THEORETICAL RELATION OF MAS AND ABSOLUTE FISHER

We take a closer look at the definition of the importance of MAS. Recall that we use the predicted probability distribution of the network to measure sensitivity rather than logits. Recall that the importance is measured at the end of training, so that our assumption is justified if the task has been learned successfully. This shows the similarity between MAS and the Absolute Fisher. The only difference is a factor of 2 **q** _X_ ( _y_ 0), which we can assume to be approximately constant as the model learned to classify training images confidently. Even with a pessimistic guess that **q** _X_ ( _y_ 0) is in a range of 0.5 (rather inconfident) and 1.0 (absolutely confident), the two measures would be highly correlated.

The data also shows that the resulting correlation between MAS and AF is large (between 0.9-0.97 on MNIST, >0.99 on CIFAR), confirming that MAS approximates AF.

**Continual Learning Performance Experiment** As an additional check of our hypothesis, we implemented continual learning baselines based on MAS, MAS-MAX, AF and compared their performances. The results in Table 1(1) show that all baselines have the same performance, further supporting the claim that MAS is effective because it approximates the Absolute Fisher.

## 5 SYNAPTIC INTELLIGENCE APPROXIMATES (ONLINE) ABSOLUTE FISHER

Here, we explain why the importance of SI is related to the Absolute Fisher, despite the apparent contrast between SI’s path integral and the Fisher Information. The theoretical part first identifies the bias of SI when approximating the path integral. It then relates the bias to the Absolute Fisher. As before, we carefully design baselines to validate each assumption of our analysis empirically.

## 5.1 BIAS OF SYNAPTIC INTELLIGENCE

To calculate _ω_ (SI) (equation 3), we need to calculate the product _p_ = _∂L∂θ_ ( _t_ ) _·_ ∆( _t_ ) for each _t_ . Evaluating the full gradient _[∂L] ∂θ_[is too expensive, so SI uses a stochastic minibatch gradient.][The] estimate of _p_ is biased since the same minibatch is used for the update ∆ and the estimate of _∂L/∂θ_ . We now give the calculations detailing this argument. For ease of exposition, let us assume vanilla SGD with learning rate 1 is used. Given a minibatch, denote its gradient estimate by _g_ + _σ_ , where _g_ = _∂L/∂θ_ denotes the full gradient and _σ_ the noise. The update is ∆= _g_ + _σ_ . Thus, _p_ should be _p_ = _g ·_ ( _g_ + _σ_ ) _._ However, using _g_ + _σ_ , which was used for the parameter update, to estimate _g_ results in _pbias_ = ( _g_ + _σ_ )[2] _._ Thus, the gradient noise introduces a bias of E[ _σ_[2] + _σg_ ] = E[ _σ_[2] ] _._

Observe that this estimate multiplies the parameter-update ∆( _t_ ) with nothing but stochastic gradient noise. From the perspective of the SI path-integral, this should be meaningless and perform poorly. Our theory, detailed below, predicts differently.

## 5.2 RELATION OF BIAS OF SI TO ABSOLUTE FISHER

An alternative way to arrive at equation 8 is following the analysis of Balles & Hennig (2018), who show that the update of Adam often depends on the sign of the moment _mt_ rather than its magnitude.

**Summary.** equation 8 shows that SI approximates the _Online Absolute Fisher_ , which averages the (empirical) Absolute Fisher ‘online’ along the training trajectory of the parameters rather than evaluating it only at the final parameter-point. We point out one subtle difference between equation 8 and AF, namely that equation 8 takes absolute values of mini-batch gradients rather than single-image gradients (like AF). In Appendix G.2 we show why this is negligible under our Assumption 1 of large gradient noise. Empirically, we also find it to be negligible, as we show below that a version of EWC based on the batched empirical Fisher has the same performance as EWC itself.

**Relation of OnAF to AF.** To see if SI, like MAS, is related to the Absolute Fisher (AF), it remains to explore the relation of OnAF and AF. If the latter two are similar, it is clear from the above that SI is similar to AF. Theoretically, in the limit of small learning rates or small Hessian (of the loss), OnAF and AF become equal. While neither of these assumption strictly hold in practice, we empirically observed - to a possibly surprising extend - strong correlations between AF and OnAF (Figure J.2). This observation still raises the question whether OnAFs relation to AF is not decreased by gradients early in training. Indeed, we present two experiments in line with this intuition in Appendix E, showing that putting more weight on later gradients can improve performance.

**Influence of Regularisation** The derivation above assumes that the parameter update ∆( _t_ ) is given by the gradients of the current task, ignoring the auxiliary regularisation loss. The latter will change update direction and momentum of the optimizer. Thus, strong regularisation will make the relation between SI and OnAF noisy, but not abolish it. This will be confirmed empirically in Section 5.3.

## 5.3 EMPRICAL INVESTIGATION OF SI, ITS BIAS AND ABSOLUTE FISHER

**Bias dominates SI & checking Assumption 1.** According to the motivation of SI the sum of importances (over parameters)[�] _i[ω]_[˜] _[i]_[(SI)][ should track the decrease in loss] _[ L]_[(0)] _[ −][L]_[(] _[T]_[)][, see equation 2.] Therefore, we investigated the summed importances for SI, its unbiased version SIU and for an approximation of the path integral based on the full training set gradient (rather than approximating it). The results, Figure 2 (left) show: (1) the bias is 5-6 times larger than the unbiased part; (2) using an unbiased gradient estimate and the full gradient gives almost identical sums, supporting the validity of the unbiased estimator. Note that even the unbiased first order approximation of the path integral overestimates the decrease in loss. This is consistent with previous studies showing that the loss has positive curvature (Jastrzebski et al., 2018; Lan et al., 2019). We emphasise that the bias (difference between SI and SIU) in Figure 2 (left) is due to the term (1 _− β_ 1) _σt_[2][and that this bias] is considerably larger than the unbiased part. This is direct, strong evidence of Assumption 1 (see Appendix G.1 for full calculation). Additionally, a detailed analysis of the gradient noise in Appendix H gives further support for Assumption 1. Results on CIFAR are analogous (Appendix Figure J.6 ).

**Bias explains SI’s performance.** We saw that the bias is much larger than the unbiased part. But how does it influence SI’s performance? To quantify this, we compared SI to its unbiased version SIU and the bias-only version SIB. Note that SIB is completely independent of the path integral motivating SI, only measures gradient noise and therefore should perform poorly according to SI’s original motivation. However, the results in Table 1(2), reveal the opposite: Removing the bias reduces performance of SI (SIU is worse), whereas isolating the bias does not affect or slightly improve performance (difference between SIB and SI on CIFAR is statistically significant, _p <_ 0 _._ 005 two-sided t-test). This demonstrates that SI relies on its bias for its continual learning performance.

**Bias and Fisher & Checking Assumption 2.** As additional experiment, we checked how much the relation of SI to the Fisher depends on the bias. Our theory predicts that the relation between SI and OnAF is mostly based on the bias. Figure 2 (mid) confirms this, showing a strong correlation between SI, SIB and OnAF and a weaker correlation between SIU and OnAF. Note that the correlation of OnAF and SI clearly supports Assumption 2; results on CIFAR are similar (c.f. Figure J.1), but with a stronger influence of regularisation as seen in Figure 2 (right) and full text below.

**Relation of SI, OnAF to AF.** Next, we investigated the relation between OnAF and AF, finding strong correlations: between 0.73-0.87 on MNIST and 0.86-0.95 on CIFAR, see Figure J.2. Figure 2 (mid panel for MNIST, right panel for CIFAR, blue) shows that this carries over to relating SI and AF. An additional control in Appendix J.2.1 shows that this observation is not an artefact of long training times. These results confirm that SI is closely related to AF.
We clarify that here we used SI importances before the division in equation 4). We show in Appendix J that the division has close to no effect on the correlations of SI with AF.

We also show in Appendix E that putting more weight on latter summands of SI, limiting the influence of early gradients, slightly improves performance, indicating that making SI more similar to AF at the final parameter point is beneficial.

**Effect of Regularisation on SI.** Next, we assessed our derivation that large regularisation will diminish the correlation between SI and OnAF. To this end, we compared standard SI to two variants of it which have less strong regularisation. The first control simply sets regularisation strength to _c_ = 0. **Empirical, batched Fisher** We demonstrated that SI is related to the OnAF and pointed out that OnAF uses the Empirical Fisher and averages across batches before taking absolute values. Here, we investigate replacing the Fisher Information in EWC by the Empirical Fisher and averaging it across a mini-batch. We refer to this algorithm Batch-EF. A theoretical analysis (Appendix G.2) suggests that with moderate batch size, Batch-EF is close to EF. Empirically, we find that EF, Batch-EF and EWC have the same performance, see Table 1 (4), confirming the theoretical prediction. In this context, we note that on P-MNIST the number of samples used to approximate the Fisher and analogous quantities has some surprising effects on performance. See C.1.1 for details.

## 6 RELATION BETWEEN ABSOLUTE FISHER AND REAL FISHER

It remains to investigate the relation of Absolute and Real Fisher. Recall that (taking expectations over inputs and predicted labels) the diagonal Fisher is defined as F = E[ _|g_ + _σ|_[2] ] while the diagonal Absolute Fisher is defined as AF = E[ _|g_ + _σ|_ ], where _g_ + _σ_ is the (stochastic) gradient.

Empirically, we see that AF and F are highly correlated, see Figure 3 left.

Theoretically, the exact relationship of Fisher and Absolute Fisher depends on the gradient distribution. If, for example, gradients are distributed normally ( _g_ + _σ_ ) _∼N_ ( _µ,_ Σ) with Σ _i,i ≫ µi_ (corresponding to the observation that the noise is much bigger than the gradients), we obtain F _i ≈_ Σ _i,i_ and (since _|g_ + _σ|_ follows a folded normal distribution) we also have AF _i ≈ c[√]_ Σ _ii_ with _c_ = �2 _/π_ . Thus, in the case of a normal distribution with large noise, we have F _[∝] ∼_ (AF)[2] . The relationship F _∝_ (AF)[2] is in fact what we empirically observe on Split CIFAR, where correlations between (AF)[2] and F are _>_ 0 _._ 98 for all tasks and repetitions, and partly on P-MNIST, where correlations between (AF)[2] and F are between 0.86 and 0.96.

Figure 3: **Empirical Relation between Absolute Fisher and Real Fisher. Left** : Note y-axis range. Summary of Pearson Correlations (top: CIFAR, bottom: MNIST), error bars represent std over 4 runs. **Mid - Right** : Example scatter plots. Relation on Split CIFAR is nearly quadratic across tasks and repetitions. There is more variation on P-MNIST (two extremes shown). Yellow lines show least-squares fit of the model _f_ ( _x_ ) = _ax_[2] with parameter _a_ .

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2020

## 7 EXPERIMENTAL SETUP

We outline the experimental setup, see Appendix C for details. We closely follow SI’s setting (Zenke et al., 2017): In domain-incremental **Permuted MNIST** (Goodfellow et al., 2013) each of 10 tasks consists of a random (but fixed) pixel-permutation of MNIST and a **fully connected ReLU network** is used. The task-incremental **Split CIFAR 10/100** (Zenke et al., 2017) consists of six 10-way classification tasks, the first is CIFAR 10, and the other ones are extracted from CIFAR 100. The **keras default** CIFAR 10 **convolutional architecture** (with dropout and max-pooling) is used keras. The only difference to Zenke et al. (2017) is that like Swaroop et al. (2019), we usually re-initialise network weights after each task, observing better performance. Code is available on github. For brevity, we partly showed plots for only one benchmark, see appenidx for analogous plots.

## 8 DISCUSSION

We have investigated regularisation approaches for continual learning, which are the method of choice for continual learning without replaying old data or expanding the model. We have provided strong theoretical and experimental evidence that the three most influential methods in this domain are surprisingly similar. This does not only unify these three methods, but also their many follow ups. While the Absolute Fisher has no clear theoretical interpretation itself, its similarity to the Fisher provides a more plausible explanation of the effectiveness of MAS- and SI based algorithms.

Moreover, our algorithm SIU to approximate SI’s path integral can used for the (non continual learning) algorithm LCA (Lan et al., 2019), which relies on a computationally expensive approximation of the same integral. This opens up new opportunities to apply LCA to larger models and datasets.

**Practical Relevance and Experimental Predictions.** Some may wonder what the use of understanding algorithms is. On top of the intrinsic theoretical benefit of understanding similarities between methods, our framework allows to predict in which situations algorithms will perform well and how they can be improved. Let us give one concrete example, that is relevant as distributed large batch training becomes more widespread: Our analysis reveals that SI is related to the Fisher through its bias, which is caused by gradient noise. Thus, increasing the batchsize, which decreases noise, will harm SI’s performance according to our theory. We tested this specific prediction and found it to be true: With a batchsize of 2048 on P-MNIST, SI’s performance degrades to 95 _._ 6 _±_ 0 _._ 1, while OnAF (with the same, large batchsize) retains 97 _._ 0 _±_ 0 _._ 1. On Split CIFAR, SI degrades to 70 _._ 0 _±_ 0 _._ 9, while OnAF retains 74 _._ 5 _±_ 0 _._ 6. We emphasise that this phenomenon is not simply explained by the change in ‘training regime’ (see e.g. Mirzadeh et al. (2020)), since SI, as predicted by our theory, is affected much more than OnAF by the increase in batch size. To summarise, our theory not only predicts degradation of SI correctly but also offers a well-performing alternative.

We now describe other predictions to exemplify the use of our framework and leave testing these and discovering further ones to future work. A commonly used optimisation technique is learning rate decay. Its effect on SI is that it puts more weights on those summands of SI’s importance (see equation 3), which correspond to larger learning rates. Large learning rates occur early in training, thus making SI’s sum less similar to the AF at the end of training.[3] Thus, we predict that learning rate decay has a negative effect on SI. In fact, we present two experiments in Appendix E showing that putting more weight on the latter summands of SI is beneficial, in line with our prediction. Interestingly, learning rate decay may be the reason that a recent empirical large-scale comparison of regularisation methods (De Lange et al., 2019) tended to observe worse performance of SI than MAS. Another somewhat puzzling observation made by De Lange et al. (2019) is that SI is more sensitive than other methods to the order in which datasetsets are presented, in cases where dataset sizes vary. In these experiments, smaller datasets are trained for fewer iterations (due to a fixed number of epochs), meaning that the sum of SI (think of OnAF) contains few summands, and is systematically smaller than for larger datasets. Thus, when first learning small datasets, SI will likely forget these since they have small importances. Starting with large datasets will not have this effect and we would expect SI to perform better. This is precisely the empirical finding in (De Lange et al., 2019).

So far, studies providing a better understanding of regularisation algorithms and their similarities had been neglected. Our contribution fills this gap and, as exemplified above, proves to be a useful tool to predict, understand and improve these algorithms.
> 3In the original framework of SI this is intended, while our framework predicts that it is harmful.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper is highly compelling, as it tackles an essential "meta-scientific" problem in the field of continual learning: the lack of theoretical cohesion among the dominant empirical regularisation methods. By establishing that EWC, SI, and MAS are fundamentally tied to the Fisher Information (or its variants), the authors move the discourse from a collection of competing heuristics to a unified theoretical framework. The writing is clear, the motivation is well-situated within the existing literature, and the contribution—identifying a previously unknown bias in SI—is statistically significant and practically useful. The paper’s greatest strength lies in its potential to streamline the field; however, the most significant risk in the subsequent sections will be the empirical validation. Given the central claim that these methods collapse into similar forms, the authors must ensure that their analysis is robust enough to account for the performance discrepancies often observed in practice. If the empirical section effectively translates these theoretical insights into improved, more justified baselines as promised, this work will likely serve as a foundational reference for future continual learning research.

comments.title: Mathematical and Notational Inconsistencies in SI and MAS Definitions
comments.explanation: Several sections contain critical mathematical errors and ambiguities: (1) The index notation for importance summation in Section 3.2 is circular and underspecified. (2) The SI importance definition is missing proper per-parameter accounting and shows potential sign errors in the path integral calculation. (3) The MAS importance definition is missing from the text snippet (OCR error), and the provided description uses non-standard vector-norm notation that conflates gradient sensitivity with Fisher information. (4) The expression for the gradient in Section 16 is typographically broken.

comments.title: Technical Flaws in Stochastic Gradient Noise and Bias Derivations
comments.explanation: The analysis of gradient noise bias contains multiple issues: (1) The simplification of E[σ² + σg] to E[σ²] erroneously disregards the dependence between stochastic noise and the underlying gradient in SGD. (2) The Adam update approximation in Section 5.2 omits the learning rate and sign, creating a directional error. (3) Assumption 1 regarding noise dominance is notationally undefined, failing to distinguish between variance and magnitude, which impacts the validity of the derivation.

comments.title: Unsupported Theoretical Claims regarding 'Bias' and Fisher Relationships
comments.explanation: The authors make several overstretched claims: (1) Characterizing the SI mechanism simply as a 'bias' is a rhetorically heavy framing that obscures the original intent of the method. (2) The claimed quadratic relationship between the Fisher and Absolute Fisher information (F ≈ AF² * π/2) assumes a zero-mean Gaussian distribution, failing to account for the signal component that becomes significant as training progresses; the authors do not justify the persistence of this relationship.

comments.title: Ambiguity regarding Methodological Scope (Online vs. Offline)
comments.explanation: The text conflates the 'Absolute Fisher' evaluation—referring at once to static, post-training, and trajectory-based (Online) variants. This leads to confusion regarding which regularizers are truly online (like SI) and which are offline (like MAS), potentially misleading the reader about the scope and compatibility of the methods discussed.

comments.title: Vague Definition and Verification of 'Batch-EF'
comments.explanation: The term 'Batch-EF' is introduced without a precise mathematical definition. Given that it claims performance parity with the empirical Fisher, the lack of clarity regarding whether it is an average of gradients or a mean of squared gradients prevents independent verification of the claim.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in parameter importance summation
comments.quote: Usually, $\omega_i^{(k)} = \omega_i^{(k-1)} + \omega_i$, where $\omega_i$ is the importance for the most recently learned task $k$.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the authors define $\omega_i^{(k)}$ as the importance after task $k$, and $\omega_i^{(k-1)}$ as the importance after task $k-1$. However, they then add $\omega_i$, which is defined as the importance for the task $k$. If $k$ is the current task, then $\omega^{(k-1)}$ should be the accumulated importance, and $\omega_i$ (the contribution of task $k$) would be $\omega_i^{(k)} - \omega_i^{(k-1)}$. Using $\omega_i^{(k)}$ on both sides (implicitly, by index $k$) creates a circular definition if read strictly. While the intent—that total importance is an accumulation over tasks—is clear to a practitioner, the indexing notation $\omega_i^{(k)} = \omega_i^{(k-1)} + \omega_i$ is mathematically loose and potentially confusing.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Taylor expansion approximation
comments.quote: $\tilde{\omega}_i^{(SI)} \approx \int_0^T \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta_i} \dot{\theta}_i dt \approx L(0) - L(T)$
comments.explanation: The authors perform a derivation where the integral of the gradient over time is approximated as the difference in loss. This is a first-order Taylor expansion application, but it assumes the loss function $L$ is a function of $\theta$ and that $\frac{dL}{dt} = \sum \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta_i} \dot{\theta}_i$. While this is a standard heuristic in the cited Zenke et al. (2017) paper, the leap from the integral form to the discrete sum is non-trivial if the trajectory is not linear. The paper proceeds to define importances based on this approximation without addressing the potential error introduced by the non-linear trajectory of parameters in deep learning.

comments.title: Overstated claim regarding unification
comments.quote: Together, (a) and (b) show that all three regularisation approaches (and their follow-ups) are closely linked to the Fisher Information.
comments.explanation: The authors claim a 'unification' of regularization approaches, asserting that SI and MAS are linked to the Fisher Information via a 'bias'. While their theoretical analysis of the gradient noise bias is insightful, characterizing the primary mechanism of SI as a 'bias' relative to an 'unbiased' ideal is a strong rhetorical framing. In actual practice, the 'bias' is often the intended signal (the path integral). Calling it a bias relative to the Fisher Information is valid from their specific analytical perspective, but might exaggerate how much this provides a 'solid theoretical justification' for the original methods as they were intended by their creators.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in the definition of parameter importance for SI
comments.quote:
To do so, we write $\Delta i ( t ) = ( \theta i ( t + 1) − \theta i ( t ))$ for an approximation of $\theta i[′] [(] [t]_[)] [dt]_[ and get]

Thus, we have $[\omega] _[i] _[(] _[SI]_[)] _[ \approx] [L] _[(0)] _[ −] [L] _[(] _[T]_[)]
comments.explanation: The text attempts a summation over the trajectory, but the notation $\Delta i (t)$ refers to a single parameter change, whereas the resulting importance $\omega_i$ in Synaptic Intelligence (SI) is typically a scalar value representing the total accumulated importance for parameter $i$ after task completion. The expression $\omega_i \approx L(0) - L(T)$ is dimensionally and conceptually wrong; $L(0) - L(T)$ is the total loss decrease for the entire model across all parameters, not for a single parameter $\theta_i$. In the original SI paper, the importance $\omega_i$ is defined as the integral of the path contributions normalized by the squared displacement to prevent divisions by zero. Representing the importance simply as the total loss decrease is a misleading oversimplification that ignores the per-parameter contribution tracking.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of MAS importance
comments.quote: Denoting, as before, the final layer of learned probabilities by $\mathbf{q} _X$ , the MAS importance is implemented as (using a euclidean norm)
comments.explanation: The passage introduces the importance measure for MAS but the equation itself is missing from the provided text snippet (the line ends abruptly after the parenthetical remark). While I recognize this is an OCR extraction, the text fails to provide the definition of the MAS importance $\Omega$ (or $\omega$), which is critical given that the section immediately following discusses its relation to the Absolute Fisher. Without the mathematical definition, the subsequent theoretical derivation in Section 4.1 remains unverifiable.

comments.title: Erroneous notation in derivative expression
comments.quote: Let us assume that we minimize the negative log-likelihood $\log \mathbf{q} _X ( y )$ and $\mathbf{[q]} _[X] _[(] _[y] _[)] write $g ( X, y ) = −\partial \log \partial \theta$ for its gradient.
comments.explanation: The notation $\partial \log \partial \theta$ is mathematically nonsensical. It is clearly intended to be $\partial \log \mathbf{q}_X(y) / \partial \theta$. Given the context of Fisher Information definitions, the reader can infer the intent, but the expression as written is a significant typographical error that obscures the definition of the gradient $g(X, y)$.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in SI gradient noise derivation
comments.explanation: In Section 5.1, the authors calculate the bias introduced by using a stochastic minibatch gradient for both the update and the loss estimate. They write p = g * (g + σ), and p_bias = (g + σ)^2. Expanding p_bias gives g^2 + 2gσ + σ^2. The difference (bias) is p_bias - p = (g^2 + 2gσ + σ^2) - (g^2 + gσ) = gσ + σ^2. Taking the expectation, E[gσ + σ^2] = g * E[σ] + E[σ^2]. Given that σ is the noise of a stochastic gradient (typically zero-mean), E[σ] = 0, so the bias is E[σ^2]. However, the authors state the bias is E[σ^2 + σg], which appears to be a algebraic transcription error regarding the cross-term simplification. While it reaches the correct final term E[σ^2], the intermediate expression is formally incorrect as written.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Adam update approximation
comments.quote: Ignoring minor normalisations and the learning rate, the parameter update is ∆( t ) = mt / ( √vt + ϵ )
comments.explanation: In Section 5.2, the authors define the Adam update as ∆(t) proportional to mt / sqrt(vt). While this is a standard approximation for the step direction, the 'ignore' clause is quite broad. In Adam, mt is a bias-corrected moment estimate (m_hat = m / (1 - beta^t)). By ignoring the bias correction terms and the learning rate, the resulting formula is dimensionally inconsistent with the actual update rule used in implementations (like the one they are analyzing). Since the argument relies on the relationship between mt and the stochastic gradient, ignoring the time-dependent bias correction factors could potentially invalidate the claim that the update is dominated by noise, especially in early training steps where bias correction is significant.

comments.title: Overstated claim regarding the 'Bias-Only' interpretation
comments.quote: From the perspective of the SI path-integral, this should be meaningless and perform poorly. Our theory, detailed below, predicts differently.
comments.explanation: The authors suggest that their theory predicts a 'Bias-Only' version of SI would perform well (implying the bias is the true source of importance). However, the subsequent claims in Section 5.2 explicitly show that the bias effectively approximates the Absolute Fisher. It is misleading to frame this as the bias being 'meaningful' in the context of the original SI path-integral intuition (which was to measure loss decrease contribution). The authors are replacing the original intuition with a different Fisher-based one; the prose masks this pivot by implying that the original method is better justified than it actually is.

comments.title: Possible Sign Error in SI Importance Path Integral
comments.explanation: In Section 3.3, the authors define the SI importance path integral based on the decrease in loss: L(0) - L(T). However, the preceding derivation at the top of page 4 defines the i-th summand via (theta_i(t+1) - theta_i(t)) * (-dL/dtheta_i). The gradient dL/dtheta_i points in the direction of *increase*. Therefore, the term (theta_i(t+1) - theta_i(t)) * (-dL/dtheta_i) is the *negative* of the change in weight times the gradient, which corresponds to the negative of the change in loss. Summing these should equate to the increase in loss (L(T) - L(0)), not the decrease (L(0) - L(T)). While the authors might be assuming the gradient descent step accounts for this, the sign of the approximation relative to the integral of the decrease is confusing and potentially inconsistent.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Absolute Fisher' definition for Online vs. Offline
comments.quote: equation 8 shows that SI approximates the _Online Absolute Fisher_ , which averages the (empirical) Absolute Fisher ‘online’ along the training trajectory of the parameters rather than evaluating it only at the final parameter-point.
comments.explanation: The text uses 'Absolute Fisher' (AF) to refer to both the final-point evaluation and the Online Absolute Fisher (OnAF) interchangeably in Section 5.3. While Section 5.3 clarifies that OnAF relates to AF, the claim in Section 1 and Section 4 that MAS is related to 'Absolute Fisher' (singular) without mentioning the trajectory-dependency can be misleading, as MAS is traditionally an offline (post-training) heuristic, whereas SI is inherently an online process. A reader might incorrectly assume MAS is also an online trajectory-based method.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for importance update
comments.quote: Usually, _ωi_[(] _[k]_[)] = _ωi_[(] _[k][−]_[1)] + _ωi_ , where _ωi_ is the importance for the most recently learned task _k_ .
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the authors use _ωi_(k) = _ωi_(k-1) + _ωi_ to define the accumulation of importance. However, in most literature (and the later SI discussion), importance is normalized or rescaled between tasks. The definition given implies simple summation without mentioning decay or normalization, which is standard for EWC but differs from how SI handles its 'Omega' values. While this is likely an abstraction, it is potentially underspecified given that SI is known for specific normalization schemes (e.g., eq 4 on p.4).

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of the parameter update Δ(t)
comments.quote: Thus, Δ(t) = m_t / (√v_t + ϵ)
comments.explanation: In Section 5.2, the authors define the Adam parameter update as Δ(t) = m_t / (√v_t + ϵ). In the standard Adam algorithm (Kingma & Ba, 2014), the update rule applied to parameters is θ_t+1 = θ_t - η * m_hat_t / (sqrt(v_hat_t) + epsilon), where m_hat and v_hat are bias-corrected moments. The authors explicitly write Δ(t) = θ(t+1) - θ(t), which would imply Δ(t) = -η * m_hat_t / (sqrt(v_hat_t) + epsilon). By omitting the learning rate η and the negative sign, the equation implies an update in the direction of the gradient rather than the descent direction, which is technically incorrect for an optimization update. While this may be a simplification, it affects the sign of the derived 'contribution' in the SI path-integral, potentially confusing a reader trying to follow the derivation of the bias term.

comments.title: Unjustified simplification of the gradient noise expectation
comments.quote: Thus, the gradient noise introduces a bias of E[ σ^2 + σg ] = E[ σ^2 ]
comments.explanation: While this aligns with the assumption that E[σ] = 0, the jump from E[σ^2 + σg] to E[σ^2] is stated as a derivation. The implication is that the cross-term E[σg] vanishes. This is only true if the minibatch gradient estimate σ is independent of the current full gradient g. However, in stochastic gradient descent, the noise σ = (g_minibatch - g) is by definition dependent on g. While I understand the authors' intent to isolate the variance of the noise, the formal treatment of σ as an independent variable here is mathematically shaky without explicitly stating that the dependence is negligible or being evaluated under a specific (e.g., Gaussian) assumption about the gradient distribution.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Batch-EF' definition
comments.quote: We refer to this algorithm Batch-EF. A theoretical analysis (Appendix G.2) suggests that with moderate batch size, Batch-EF is close to EF.
comments.explanation: The authors refer to 'Batch-EF' and claim it performs similarly to EF and EWC. However, the exact definition of 'Batch-EF' is not provided in the text; it is only implied to be the empirical Fisher averaged across a mini-batch. Because the Fisher Information is defined as the expectation over the data distribution, an 'empirical' Fisher usually refers to the sample covariance of gradients. Averaging the *gradient* across a mini-batch before taking the square (which is what I interpret as the 'batch' approach) leads to a different quantity than the average of the squared gradients (the standard empirical Fisher). This ambiguity makes the 'theoretical claim' difficult to independently verify without the omitted appendix.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Mathematical Derivation of MAS Importance
comments.quote: ωi(MAS) = E_X∼X_ [|| ∂q_X / ∂θi ||²₂]
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the authors define MAS importance using the squared Euclidean norm of the gradient of the predicted probability vector q_X with respect to parameter θi. However, q_X is a vector of probabilities over labels, so ∂q_X / ∂θi is a vector of dimension |L|. The term || · ||²_2 in the expression implies a norm over the label space, resulting in a scalar for each θi. This is consistent with the definition of the Fisher Information (where we sum over predicted classes). However, the mathematical formalism in the paper conflates the sensitivity of the entire distribution (a vector) with the gradient of the log-likelihood (a scalar used in EWC/Fisher). While the intuition is clear, the notation for 'sensitivity' across a probability distribution usually involves a sum over labels, not just a norm of the vector of derivatives itself, unless it is implicitly assumes the norm is the Fisher metric. This creates ambiguity whether they are taking the norm of the gradient vector (which includes all labels), which aligns with the empirical implementation but deviates slightly from the mathematical definition of parameter-wise importance used in standard regularisation literature.

comments.title: Ambiguity in SI Gradient Noise Assumption
comments.quote: (1 - β1)σt² >> β1 mt-1 gt
comments.explanation: The authors define the bias condition in Assumption 1 as (1 - β1)σt² >> β1 mt-1 gt. There is a dimensional mismatch/notation inconsistency here: σt is typically a standard deviation (noise magnitude), while gt is the gradient. If σt denotes the variance of the gradient noise, the inequality holds; if it denotes the noise itself, the notation σt² is correct but the comparison to a product involving gt without a squaring operation or index alignment across the entire equation is sloppy. Given that β1 and (1-β1) are scalars, the equation suggests a relationship between variances. While the intent is to show that noise dominates the update logic, the notation is mathematically undefined without clarifying if these are variances or squared magnitudes of stochastic vectors.

comments.title: Questionable Claim regarding Fisher/Absolute Fisher proportionality
comments.quote: F_i ≈ (AF_i)² * (π/2)
comments.explanation: The paper claims a fundamental quadratic relationship between the Fisher and Absolute Fisher (F approx AF^2 * pi/2). While this holds under the assumption that the gradient distribution is Gaussian with zero mean (N(0, Σ)), the authors acknowledge in Assumption 3 that (g + σ) ~ N(μ, Σ). If μ ≠ 0 (i.e., there is a non-zero gradient), the relationship F = E[g^2] and AF = E[|g|] changes significantly from the pi/2 factor derived from the folded normal distribution. The claim that this holds 'empirically across benchmarks' is strong, but the mathematical justification relies on the 'high noise' assumption where the signal (gradient) is negligible compared to the variance. The paper does not sufficiently address how this relationship degrades as training progresses and the gradient (signal) becomes more informative/larger, which would theoretically alter the pi/2 constant.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper presents a rigorous and insightful theoretical unification of three prominent regularization-based continual learning methods—EWC, SI, and MAS—demonstrating that all three are fundamentally linked to the Fisher Information. The work is notable for its clarity of exposition, careful experimental design, and the identification of a previously unrecognized bias in SI that explains its empirical performance. These contributions are significant, as they provide a more principled foundation for a widely used class of algorithms and offer practical improvements, such as more justified versions of MAS and SI. However, the paper could be strengthened by more explicitly addressing the limitations of its unification: while the theoretical connections are convincing, the practical implications for algorithm selection or performance gains in diverse continual learning scenarios remain somewhat abstract. Additionally, the paper would benefit from a more detailed discussion of how the proposed "Absolute Fisher" variant differs from the standard Fisher Information in terms of computational cost and empirical behavior, especially given that diagonal approximations are known to be crude. ## High-Level Feedback

This paper presents a rigorous and insightful theoretical unification of three prominent regularization-based continual learning methods—EWC, SI, and MAS—demonstrating that all three are fundamentally linked to the Fisher Information. The work is notable for its clarity of exposition, careful experimental design, and the identification of a previously unrecognized bias in SI that explains its empirical performance. These contributions are significant, as they provide a more principled foundation for a widely used class of algorithms and offer practical improvements, such as more justified versions of MAS and SI. However, the paper could be strengthened by more explicitly addressing the limitations of its unification: while the theoretical connections are convincing, the practical implications for algorithm selection or performance gains in diverse continual learning scenarios remain somewhat abstract. Additionally, the paper would benefit from a more detailed discussion of how the proposed "Absolute Fisher" variant differs from the standard Fisher Information in terms of computational cost and empirical behavior, especially given that diagonal approximations are known to be crude. Finally, while the abstract and introduction are well-structured, the related work section is dense and could be more selectively focused on positioning this paper's contributions relative to the most directly comparable efforts at theoretical unification. Overall, this is a high-quality, impactful paper with strong theoretical and empirical support, but its significance would be enhanced by clearer articulation of the practical boundaries of its claims.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript tackles a highly relevant problem by proposing a unifying theoretical framework for three influential regularization-based continual learning methods—EWC, SI, and MAS—arguing that their parameter importance measures are fundamentally tied to the Fisher Information, despite originating from distinct motivations. The core insight that SI's empirical effectiveness may be attributed to a bias structurally linked to the Fisher Information is particularly novel and, if supported by rigorous analysis, offers substantial value by demystifying existing heuristics and guiding more principled algorithm development. However, the presentation suffers from immediate clarity and polish issues: the title contains a jarring typo ("CONTIN UAL"), and the abstract introduces the term "Absolute Fisher" without definition. Furthermore, the Related Work section claims that no prior attention has been directed toward unifying or theoretically understanding these methods; the authors must carefully verify this assertion, as connections between MAS output sensitivity and Fisher curvature, as well as approximations in SI, have been discussed in prior literature, and overstating novelty risks misrepresenting the state of the field. Finally, the characterization of SI's mechanism as a "bias" that drives performance requires precise formalization, as the terminology is counter-intuitive and could confuse readers if not clearly distinguished from standard bias-variance trade-offs or framed as a beneficial correction term.

comments.title: Misuse of 'methods' instead of 'tasks' in Replay methods description
comments.quote: Replay methods refer to algorithms which either store a small sample or generate data of old distributions and use this data while training on new methods
comments.explanation: Upon reading this sentence, I initially paused at the phrase 'training on new methods.' In the context of continual learning, the sequential objective is to learn from a stream of new tasks or data distributions, not new algorithms. I checked the surrounding context, particularly the formal definition of continual learning provided later in the paper ('given K datasets D1...DK sequentially'), which confirms that 'tasks' is the standard and intended term here. The word 'methods' is almost certainly a typo for 'tasks'. While a knowledgeable reader can easily deduce the intent, this choice of wording is misleading and contradicts the standard terminology used throughout the rest of the passage and the field. Correcting it to 'tasks' would align with conventions and prevent temporary confusion about what is being trained on.

comments.title: Subject-verb agreement error in Abstract
comments.quote: An influential method to tackle this are so-called regularisation approaches.
comments.explanation: The subject 'An influential method' is singular, while the verb 'are' is plural. Given that the abstract immediately lists three distinct approaches (EWC, SI, MAS) and discusses them as a collective class, the author likely meant 'methods' or intended to say 'An influential line of work...'. This is a minor grammatical error that does not affect the technical argument but should be corrected for polish and clarity.

comments.title: Number agreement error in Related Work

comments.explanation: The phrase 'an existing networks' mixes a singular article with a plural noun. The context discusses architectural methods that extend or modify a given model, so the intended meaning is clearly 'an existing network'. This is a straightforward typo that does not impact the technical content but should be fixed.

comments.title: SI Importance Approximation Ambiguity
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by the mangled equation following the path-integral approximation. The text defines the i-th summand as 'the contribution of parameter θ_i to the decrease in loss'. However, the equation appears to state that this per-parameter contribution equals the total loss decrease L(0) - L(T). In standard continual learning literature, the sum over all parameters of these contributions approximates the total loss decrease, not the individual parameter's share. I checked the surrounding context and the rescaling step mentioned immediately after, which normalizes contributions across dimensions. It is highly likely that an OCR artifact dropped a summation over i (or a normalization fraction) from the equation. As written, a careful reader might incorrectly conclude that each individual parameter's importance is estimated by the total task loss decrease, which contradicts the subsequent per-parameter rescaling and bias analysis. This should be clarified to prevent conceptual misunderstanding of SI's importance decomposition.

comments.title: Precision of Fisher-Hessian Equality Claim
comments.quote: Under the assumption that the learned label distribution **q** _X_ is the real label distribution the Fisher equals the Hessian of the loss (Martens, 2014; Pascanu & Bengio, 2013).
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify a well-known theoretical relationship between the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) and the Hessian of the loss. However, the phrasing 'Fisher equals the Hessian of the loss' is slightly imprecise. Standard statistical learning theory (e.g., Martens 2014) establishes that the FIM equals the Hessian of the *expected* negative log-likelihood (or population loss), not the empirical loss computed on a finite dataset. For cross-entropy, the empirical Hessian contains noise and does not equal the FIM pointwise, even under perfect distribution matching. Acknowledging this distinction would prevent readers from incorrectly assuming $F = \nabla^2 L_{\text{data}}(\theta)$ in practical, finite-sample settings. The authors otherwise handle the FIM vs. Empirical Fisher distinction well earlier in the section, so this appears to be an oversight in wording rather than a methodological flaw.

When training on task _k_ , regularisation methods use the loss [newline] where _c >_ 0 is a hyperparameter.
[...]
Denoting, as before, the final layer of learned probabilities by **q** _X_ , the MAS importance is implemented as (using a euclidean norm) [newline]
[...]

Specifically, the explicit regularisation loss function, the exact MAS importance formula, and the derivation showing MAS's similarity to the Absolute Fisher are missing. While the surrounding prose conceptually describes these terms (e.g., noting MAS uses squared output sensitivities and the loss includes a quadratic penalty), the absence of the equations makes it impossible to verify the authors' claims about linear dependence or to check for subtle coefficient mismatches. I checked Appendix references and the surrounding text to see if these were deferred, but they are central to Sections 3, 4, and 5's theoretical contributions. If these equations are indeed missing in the final PDF, it would significantly impede reproducibility and theoretical assessment. (Likely an OCR extraction failure, but flagged as it directly impacts the verifiability of the paper's core claims.)

comments.title: Bias Derivation Notation Mixes Scalars and Vectors
comments.quote: Thus, the gradient noise introduces a bias of E[ _σ_[2] + _σg_ ] = E[ _σ_[2] ] _._
comments.explanation: In the SGD analysis of SI's bias, the authors write the bias term as $E[\sigma^2 + \sigma g]$. For multi-parameter neural networks, $g$ and $\sigma$ are vectors, and the correct notation should explicitly use the dot product (e.g., $\|\sigma\|^2 + \sigma^\top g$) or state that the derivation is component-wise. While the expectation $E[\sigma_i g_i] = 0$ under standard zero-mean noise assumptions is mathematically sound, the scalar notation used here obscures how the bias accumulates across dimensions and how it interacts with the vector-valued Adam updates analyzed in Section 5.2. Clarifying the dimensionality would strengthen the derivation and align it with the subsequent Adam-specific analysis, where component-wise normalization is implicit. This is a minor notational imprecision rather than a mathematical error.

comments.title: Vector vs. Scalar Notation in Bias Derivation
comments.quote: Thus, the gradient noise introduces a bias of E[ _σ_[2] + _σg_ ] = E[ _σ_[2] ] _.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned that the derivation in Section 5.1 treats the gradient $g$, noise $\sigma$, and their products as scalars, whereas in neural network optimization these are $N$-dimensional vectors. The expression $E[\sigma^2 + \sigma g]$ lacks explicit dot-product or element-wise notation, which could mislead a reader into thinking the bias is a simple scalar sum rather than a component-wise or norm-based quantity. Upon reflection, in continual learning literature it is common to drop vector notation when discussing diagonal approximations or per-parameter importance, and the final scalar bias $E[\sigma^2]$ aligns with the expected magnitude of the squared noise term. However, explicitly denoting element-wise operations or norms (e.g., $\sigma \odot \sigma$ or $\|\sigma\|^2$) would prevent dimensional ambiguity. This does not undermine the core argument but represents a minor clarity gap.

comments.title: Unexplained Factor of 2 in MAS–Absolute Fisher Relation
comments.quote: The only difference is a factor of 2 **q** _X_ ( _y_ 0), which we can assume to be approximately constant as the model learned to classify training images confidently.
comments.explanation: In Section 4.1, the authors claim MAS and the Absolute Fisher differ only by a factor of $2q_X(y_0)$. Using the standard chain rule on the log-likelihood gradient yields $\nabla_\theta \log q_X(y) = \frac{1}{q_X(y)} \nabla_\theta q_X(y)$, which would suggest a factor of $1/q_X(y_0)$, not $2q_X(y_0)$. I checked whether the factor 2 might arise from a specific loss formulation (e.g., cross-entropy derivative structure) or a different definition of the Absolute Fisher used elsewhere in the paper. Since Equation 5 (which contains the derivation) is missing due to OCR, I cannot verify the algebraic steps. If the factor 2 stems from a non-standard definition or an intermediate approximation, it should be explicitly stated to avoid confusion. This is a moderate logical gap that could mislead readers attempting to reproduce the theoretical link without the full derivation.

comments.title: Component-wise Inequality Notation in Assumption 1
comments.quote: Here, we made **Assumption 1** that the gradient noise is larger than the gradient, or more precisely: (1 _− β_ 1) _σt_[2] _[≫][β]_[1] _[m][t][−]_[1] _[g][t]_
comments.explanation: Assumption 1 compares $(1-\beta_1)\sigma_t^2$ against $\beta_1 m_{t-1} g_t$ using a scalar inequality symbol `\gg`. Since $\sigma_t$, $m_{t-1}$, and $g_t$ are parameter vectors, the inequality implicitly assumes a component-wise (element-wise) comparison or a norm-based bound, but this is not explicitly stated. In Adam, exponential averages and updates are computed per-parameter, so the assumption is likely intended element-wise. However, mixing squared terms and products without notation (e.g., $\sigma_t \odot \sigma_t$ or $\|\cdot\|$) leaves open whether the assumption holds in norm, max-norm, or element-wise. Clarifying the comparison mode would strengthen the precision of the assumption, though it does not invalidate the subsequent approximation logic.

comments.title: Imprecise theoretical condition for OnAF/AF equality
comments.quote: Theoretically, in the limit of small learning rates or small Hessian (of the loss), OnAF and AF become equal.
comments.explanation: The authors state that Online Absolute Fisher (OnAF) and Absolute Fisher (AF) converge when the Hessian is 'small'. However, a small Hessian implies the loss surface is locally flat, which uniformly shrinks gradient magnitudes across the entire trajectory without guaranteeing that the time-averaged trajectory (OnAF) matches the final-point evaluation (AF). The two quantities become equal primarily when the parameter trajectory is short (ensured by small learning rates) or when the gradient norm remains approximately constant along the path (e.g., linear models or constant curvature regimes). 'Small Hessian' alone does not ensure this equality; it is likely a phrasing mix-up with 'constant Hessian' or 'negligible parameter movement'. This is a logical imprecision that slightly weakens the theoretical framing, though the empirical claim of strong correlation remains unaffected.

comments.title: Figure reference mismatch regarding dataset
comments.quote: Figure 2 (mid panel for MNIST, right panel for CIFAR, blue) shows that this carries over to relating SI and AF.
comments.explanation: The main text explicitly claims the right panel of Figure 2 displays CIFAR results. However, the figure caption labels the Mid panel as MNIST and describes the Right panel only as 'Relation between standard-SI (blue) and OnAF and two SI-controls', with no dataset specification. Given standard figure layout conventions and the fact that the Mid panel shows MNIST, the Right panel almost certainly depicts MNIST results under different regularisation strengths (as supported by the caption's description of controls and the adjacent text discussing regularisation effects). The explicit reference to CIFAR in the main text appears to be a typo or mis-reference, which could mislead a careful reader attempting to locate the specific CIFAR bias/regularisation interaction plot.

comments.title: Contradictory experimental setup description regarding weight re-initialization
comments.quote: The only difference to Zenke et al. (2017) is that like Swaroop et al. (2019), we usually re-initialise network weights after each task, observing better performance.
comments.explanation: The authors state they 're-initialise network weights after each task'. In a continual learning setting, this would discard all parameters learned during task $k$ before starting task $k+1$, causing immediate catastrophic forgetting and contradicting the core goal of the paper. This likely contains a typo and should read 're-initialise optimizer state' or 're-initialise [weights] before each task'. As written, it misrepresents the methodology and undermines confidence in the reported results, as standard continual learning baselines do not re-initialize the network between tasks.

comments.title: Ambiguous vector/scalar notation in Assumption 1
comments.quote: (1 - \beta_1) \sigma_t^2 \gg \beta_1 m_{t-1} g_t
comments.explanation: Assumption 1 compares terms involving the gradient noise $\sigma_t$, previous momentum $m_{t-1}$, and current gradient $g_t$. Since these are vectors (or vectors of parameter importances per dimension), the expression mixes squares and products without specifying element-wise operations or dot products. While likely intended as element-wise inequalities per parameter, the notation is formally ambiguous and could mislead a careful reader regarding the precise condition required for the bias to dominate the signal term in the Adam momentum update.

comments.title: Minor algebraic imprecision in bias derivation
comments.quote: Thus, the gradient noise introduces a bias of E[ \sigma^2 + \sigma g ] = E[ \sigma^2 ].
comments.explanation: The authors expand the biased product as $p_{bias} = (g+\sigma)^2$, which correctly equals $g^2 + 2g\sigma + \sigma^2$. When taking expectations, the cross-term $2g\sigma$ vanishes (since $\mathbb{E}[\sigma]=0$), leaving $g^2 + \mathbb{E}[\sigma^2]$. The text references a bias term $E[\sigma^2 + \sigma g]$, omitting the factor of 2 on the cross-term. While the final conclusion ($\mathbb{E}[\sigma^2]$) is correct because the expectation of the cross-term is zero regardless of the coefficient, the intermediate algebraic step is imprecise and slightly confusing.

comments.title: Trivial typographical error
comments.quote: datasetsets
comments.explanation: A simple typographical error in Section 8 ('order in which datasetsets are presented'). While harmless to the technical content, it is a minor proofreading oversight.
